questions.question: What is the main goal of clarification in an ambiguous design prompt?
options: To eliminate every unknown before any architecture discussion begins.
To identify the uncertainties that actually change scope, workload, or guarantees.
To prove that the prompt was underspecified by the interviewer.
To delay the deep dive until the interviewer gives away the intended answer.
questions.explanation: Clarification is meant to uncover design-shaping uncertainty, not all possible uncertainty.

questions.question: Why are explicit assumptions useful?
options: They let you move forward honestly when the interviewer leaves details open.
They replace the need for capacity planning entirely.
They guarantee the interviewer will not change constraints later.
They matter only for storage-heavy prompts.
questions.explanation: Assumptions create a clear basis for the design and make later redesign easier.

questions.question: Which clarification question is usually highest leverage?
options: What exact UI library should the web client use?
Is the dominant challenge low-latency reads, high write volume, or correctness-sensitive updates?
Should the architecture diagram include icons or boxes only?
Can we postpone naming any data store until the last minute?
questions.explanation: Workload shape and guarantee priorities often change the architecture directly.

questions.question: What is the main risk of asking too many low-impact questions?
options: The design can lose momentum without becoming any more correct or better scoped.
It always forces the system into eventual consistency.
It prevents the interviewer from asking follow-up questions later.
It mainly affects diagram aesthetics.
questions.explanation: Excessive low-value questioning burns time and makes the candidate seem less decisive.

questions.question: Which assumption is strongest to state explicitly?
options: I will assume external traffic and moderate global growth because that materially affects partitioning, latency, and rollout risk.
I assume the database will be fast enough because modern databases are optimized.
I assume all failures are rare so we can focus only on the happy path.
I assume the interviewer prefers message queues to RPC.
questions.explanation: Good assumptions are concrete, defensible, and connected to architectural consequences.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with narrowing to one core workflow?
options: You gain depth and coherence, but some secondary features will be intentionally deferred.
You guarantee that the design cannot scale beyond one region.
You remove the need to discuss non-functional requirements.
You automatically make every future redesign easier.
questions.explanation: A narrow scope helps depth, but it requires explicit deferral of other concerns.

questions.question: If the interviewer changes one assumption mid-answer, what is the best response?
options: Restart from the beginning without referencing earlier design choices.
Map the changed assumption to the affected components and explain what now becomes more expensive, slower, or more complex.
Ignore the change unless it affects the database selection directly.
Defend the first assumption because consistency of narrative matters more than accuracy.
questions.explanation: Changed constraints should trigger focused redesign, not panic or stubbornness.

questions.question: Which sign most strongly suggests the prompt has been scoped enough to proceed?
options: You can name the primary user journey, main workload shape, and top non-functional priority well enough to size the system.
You have listed all optional features that might ever be requested.
You have settled every implementation detail for the write path.
The interviewer has stopped talking entirely.
questions.explanation: Once you can size and prioritize the system, further clarification has diminishing returns.
